content:
Every new funding round buys time. None of them can make an unviable business profitable.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Still from Scarface (1983)When I watch Sam Altman — what he says, what he does — I keep thinking of the final scene from the famous movie Scarface.
Remember it? Tony Montana is doomed. His mansion is surrounded by a Colombian cartel hit squad. He’s bleeding out, but he keeps firing back, reaching again and again for the cocaine to numb the agony closing in on him, if only for another minute.
Then the end comes. Riddled with bullets, Montana falls into the pool beneath the neon sign that reads The World Is Yours. The water turns red.
Every time I read another round of OpenAI news, it feels like Sam Altman is on that same road and can’t get off it. Like the character in the film, he seems to genuinely believe he can outrun fate.
I don’t mean anything mystical by that. I mean plain common sense and the laws of the market. Altman doesn’t seem to use the first and doesn’t want to think about the second.
It’s as if he’s counting, every single time, on scoring one more hit that will pull him through: another…
